storm.summary_layperson: No storms are approaching this afternoon — just hazy wildfire smoke overhead — and even the small chance of an evening shower is further reduced near the water by the cool Sound.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Cool Sound water (70°F) under warm air keeps a stable marine layer that tends to weaken storms at the coastline.
storm.detail_html:
Quiet convective afternoon. Radar shows no storm cells anywhere in the approach sector, and the atmosphere over the cove currently has essentially no storm fuel (0 J/kg CAPE). The main sky story is wildfire smoke aloft — an Air Quality Alert is in effect through midnight — with partly sunny skies underneath.

The NWS forecast keeps a slight (20%) chance of an isolated shower or thunderstorm late this afternoon into the evening. Modeled instability ticks up only marginally (about 110 J/kg by 5 PM) — enough for a stray shower, far short of what organized storms need.

The cove's usual summertime protection is active: Sound water near 70°F under 81–83°F air maintains a moderate stable marine layer along the shoreline. With so little storm fuel available, anything that pops inland would most likely weaken or split before reaching the waterfront — the cove's risk is lower than towns a few miles inland.

Winds aloft are strong (about 53 kt of deep-layer shear), which would organize storms if fuel were present — but with near-zero instability there is nothing for it to work with today.

The picture changes Saturday, when widespread showers and thunderstorms are expected. For tonight, the call would only shift if late-afternoon heating builds instability faster than modeled and a cell fires over Westchester — worth a radar glance around 6–7 PM, nothing more.

wind.regime_label: Moderate gradient wind — no sea breeze
wind.tactics_blurb: West-northwest breeze channeling down the Sound, puffy with 15–30° swings and holes near the shoreline — stay center of the course for the most consistent pressure and play the right-hand puffs. The breeze eases but holds past the 8:25 PM sunset; later starts will be light.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
This is a moderate gradient-wind day — no sea breeze in play. The air–water temperature difference (11°F: low-80s air over a 70°F Sound) would normally support a thermal sea breeze, but only a quarter of coastal stations show any southerly component and none of the Long Island north-shore stations do — the westerly background wind is channeling straight down the Sound and the sea breeze never established. The wind you feel is the large-scale gradient, around 8 kt at well-sited stations.

The Riverside YC station is reading WNW (288°) at 8 kt, gusting 13. The model veers the breeze right through the evening — west at 5 PM becoming northwest by 8 PM — and the observed trend agrees with the direction of that swing. One caveat: the model and the station network disagree by about 29° on the current direction, and agreement across stations is modest, so treat the hour-by-hour direction as ±15–20°.

West-to-WNW flow reaches the cove channeled along the Sound's axis: direction is fairly steady, but the Connecticut shoreline leaves holes close in, with better pressure toward the middle of the course. As the breeze veers toward northwest it turns puffier and shiftier — 15–30° swings with distinct puff lanes coming off the northwest shore. Stay center for the best average pressure and take the right-hand puffs when they come down the course.

Because this is gradient wind rather than a thermal sea breeze, it does not die with the sun. Expect it to ease from about 11 kt at 5 PM to 6–7 kt around the 8:25 PM sunset, then soften another knot or two after dark as the land cools and the gusts damp out. Races that finish before 8 PM get the best of it.

For the fleet: 6–11 kt is the one-design sweet spot — fully powered without survival conditions, gusts to 15 early in the window. The late window (after 8 PM) will be light, 5–6 kt: raceable but finicky, and light-air specialists' territory.

data_caveats: HRRR model hours beyond 6 PM failed to download; the 7–10 PM wind rows are extrapolated from the gradient-wind trend.
Buoy WLIS (western Sound) observation was 72 minutes old at fetch time.
